subject: Science
question_text: Which is a property of liquids?
worked_solution:
Liquids have no fixed shape — they flow and take the shape of their container.
Solids have fixed shape. Gases are easily compressed. All matter has mass.

wrong_explanations.A: Fixed shape is a property of solids.
wrong_explanations.B: Easily compressed is a property of gases.
wrong_explanations.D: All matter has mass.

subject: Science
question_text: Which state of matter has a fixed shape AND a fixed volume?
worked_solution:
Solids have fixed shape AND fixed volume.
Liquids have fixed volume but NOT fixed shape.
Gases have neither.

wrong_explanations.B: Liquids have fixed volume but take shape of container.
wrong_explanations.C: Gases have no fixed shape or volume.
wrong_explanations.D: Only solids have both.

subject: Science
question_text: What happens when water is heated to 100°C?
worked_solution:
Water boils at 100°C → changes from liquid to gas (water vapour).
Freezing = 0°C. Melting = solid→liquid. Condensation = gas→liquid.

wrong_explanations.A: Freezing happens at 0°C, not 100°C.
wrong_explanations.D: Condensation is gas→liquid — the reverse of boiling.

subject: Science
question_text: What is the process of a solid changing directly to a gas called?
worked_solution:
Sublimation = solid directly to gas (skipping liquid).
Example: dry ice (solid CO₂) turning into gas.

wrong_explanations.A: Evaporation is liquid→gas.
wrong_explanations.D: Melting is solid→liquid.

subject: Science
question_text: Which is an example of a gas?
worked_solution:
Water vapour is water in gas form — invisible, fills any space.
Ice = solid. Juice = liquid. Sand = solid.

wrong_explanations.A: Ice is solid water.
wrong_explanations.D: Sand is made of tiny solid particles.

subject: Science
question_text: True or False: Air has mass.
worked_solution:
Air is made of gas particles. All matter — including gases — has mass.
We can show this by weighing an inflated vs deflated balloon.

subject: Science
question_text: When water vapour cools and turns back into liquid water on a cold glass, this process is called ______.
worked_solution:
Gas cooling into liquid = condensation.
Example: water droplets forming on a cold drink glass.

subject: Science
question_text: Why do wet clothes dry when hung outside on a sunny day?
worked_solution:
Heat from the sun gives energy to water → water evaporates (liquid→gas).
The water vapour goes into the air, so clothes become dry.

wrong_explanations.A: Freezing would make the clothes stiff, not dry.
wrong_explanations.C: Condensation adds water, not removes it.
wrong_explanations.D: Melting changes solid→liquid. Wet clothes are already liquid.

subject: Science
question_text: Ahmad pours water from a tall thin glass into a short wide bowl. What happens to the volume of water?
worked_solution:
Volume of liquid stays the same regardless of container shape.
The water looks different but the amount hasn't changed.

wrong_explanations.A: Volume doesn't increase just by changing container.
wrong_explanations.B: No water is removed, so volume doesn't decrease.
wrong_explanations.D: Water doesn't disappear when poured.

subject: Science
question_text:
Which correctly shows the arrangement of particles?
Solid → Liquid → Gas
options: Close & fixed → Close & moving → Far apart & fast
No particles → Some particles → Many particles
worked_solution:
Solid: particles close together in fixed positions.
Liquid: particles close but can slide past each other.
Gas: particles far apart, moving fast in all directions.

wrong_explanations.B: This is reversed — solids are closest, gases farthest.
wrong_explanations.C: Solids are orderly, gases are random — this is reversed.
wrong_explanations.D: All states have particles. More matter doesn't mean more particles per volume.

subject: Science
question_text: Which is NOT a source of heat?
worked_solution:
A mirror reflects light and heat but does not produce its own heat.
The Sun, candles, and heaters all produce heat.

wrong_explanations.A: The Sun is a natural heat source.
wrong_explanations.B: A burning candle produces heat from the flame.
wrong_explanations.D: A heater is designed to produce heat.

subject: Science
question_text: A metal spoon in hot water gets warm. Why?
options: The spoon makes its own heat
Cold flows from spoon to water
Heat flows from hot water through the spoon
worked_solution:
Heat flows from hotter (water) to cooler (spoon).
Metal is a good conductor — heat travels through the spoon to the handle.
Keyword: Heat flows from a hotter to a cooler object.

wrong_explanations.A: Spoons don't produce heat on their own.
wrong_explanations.D: The air is cooler than the water — not the main heat source.

subject: Science
question_text: Which material is a good conductor of heat?
worked_solution:
Metals are good conductors of heat. Copper is a metal.
Wood, plastic, rubber are poor conductors (insulators).

wrong_explanations.A: Wood is a poor conductor (insulator).
wrong_explanations.D: Rubber is a poor conductor.

subject: Science
question_text: What is the difference between heat and temperature?
options: Heat is measured in °C, temperature in joules
Heat is energy that flows; temperature measures hotness
Temperature flows from hot to cold
worked_solution:
Heat is a form of energy that flows from hotter to cooler objects.
Temperature is a measurement of how hot or cold something is.
They are related but different.

wrong_explanations.A: They are not the same — heat is energy, temperature is a measurement.
wrong_explanations.B: Reversed: temperature is in °C, heat is measured in joules.
wrong_explanations.D: Heat flows, not temperature.

subject: Science
question_text: What happens to a metal ball when heated?
worked_solution:
When heated, particles gain energy and move further apart → object expands.
This is why bridges have expansion gaps.

wrong_explanations.A: Contraction happens when cooling, not heating.
wrong_explanations.C: Objects do change size when heated (expand).
wrong_explanations.D: Metals have very high melting points — they expand long before melting.

subject: Science
question_text: Why are cooking pot handles made of plastic or wood?
options: They are poor conductors so they stay cool
They conduct heat to your hands faster
worked_solution:
Plastic and wood are poor conductors of heat (insulators).
They don't transfer heat to your hands, keeping the handle cool and safe to touch.

wrong_explanations.A: Appearance is not the reason — safety is.
wrong_explanations.B: Cost is not the main reason — safety is.
wrong_explanations.D: Opposite: they conduct heat POORLY, keeping hands cool.

subject: Science
question_text: When an object gains heat, its temperature ______ (increases/decreases).
worked_solution:
Gaining heat = more energy = particles move faster = temperature rises.
Heat gain → temperature increases.

subject: Science
question_text: A thermos flask keeps drinks hot. How does it reduce heat loss?
options: By adding heat to the drink
By using metal walls that conduct heat away
By using insulating materials and a vacuum
By cooling the outside of the flask
worked_solution:
A thermos uses insulating materials and a vacuum (no air) between walls.
The vacuum prevents heat from being conducted or convected away.
This keeps the drink hot longer.

wrong_explanations.A: The flask doesn't add heat — it prevents heat loss.
wrong_explanations.B: Metal would conduct heat away, the opposite of what we want.
wrong_explanations.D: Cooling the outside doesn't keep the inside hot.

subject: Science
question_text: Which is NOT a source of light?
worked_solution:
A source of light PRODUCES its own light.
A mirror REFLECTS light but does not produce it.
Keyword: A source of light produces its own light.

wrong_explanations.A: The Sun produces its own light — it is a source.
wrong_explanations.B: A torch produces light using electricity.
wrong_explanations.D: A candle flame produces light.

subject: Science
question_text: Light travels in ______.
worked_solution:
Light travels in straight lines.
This is why shadows have sharp edges — light cannot bend around objects.

wrong_explanations.A: Light doesn't naturally curve.
wrong_explanations.C: Zigzag is not how light travels.
wrong_explanations.D: Light doesn't travel in circles.

subject: Science
question_text: What happens to a shadow when the light source is moved closer to the object?
worked_solution:
When light source moves closer → the object blocks more light → shadow gets bigger.
When light source moves further → shadow gets smaller.

wrong_explanations.A: Closer light = bigger shadow, not smaller.
wrong_explanations.C: Shadow size changes with light source distance.
wrong_explanations.D: Shadows don't disappear unless you remove the light or object.

subject: Science
question_text: Which material allows ALL light to pass through it?
worked_solution:
Transparent materials allow ALL light through (clear glass, clear water).
Translucent allows SOME light (frosted glass).
Opaque allows NO light (wood, cardboard).

wrong_explanations.B: Frosted glass is translucent — only some light passes through.
wrong_explanations.C: Wood is opaque — no light passes through.
wrong_explanations.D: Cardboard is opaque — no light passes through.

subject: Science
question_text: True or False: We can see objects in a dark room without any light source.
worked_solution:
We need light to see. Light bounces (reflects) off objects into our eyes.
In complete darkness, no light reflects → we cannot see anything.

subject: Science
question_text: A shadow is formed when light is blocked by an ______ object.
worked_solution:
Opaque objects block all light → create shadows.
Transparent objects let light through → no shadow.
Keyword: opaque.

subject: Science
question_text: Which object is attracted by a magnet?
worked_solution:
Magnets attract objects made of iron, steel, nickel, or cobalt.
Steel paper clip = made of steel (contains iron) → attracted.
Wood, glass, rubber are NOT magnetic.

wrong_explanations.A: Wood is not magnetic.
wrong_explanations.D: Rubber is not magnetic.

subject: Science
question_text: Ali tested which objects are attracted to a magnet. Which result is CORRECT?
worked_solution:
Only iron, steel, nickel, cobalt are magnetic.
Iron nail → attracted ✓.
Aluminium and copper are metals but NOT magnetic.
Plastic is not magnetic.

wrong_explanations.A: Aluminium is non-magnetic even though it's a metal.
wrong_explanations.B: Copper is non-magnetic even though it's a metal.
wrong_explanations.D: Plastic is not a magnetic material.

subject: Science
question_text: What happens when two North poles of magnets are brought together?
worked_solution:
Like poles repel: N-N repel, S-S repel.
Opposite poles attract: N-S attract.
Keyword: Like poles repel, unlike poles attract.

wrong_explanations.A: Same poles repel, not attract.
wrong_explanations.C: Magnets always interact when close enough.
wrong_explanations.D: Same poles push apart, they don't stick.

subject: Science
question_text: Why do fridge doors have magnets?
worked_solution:
Fridge door magnets attract to the metal fridge body.
This keeps the door sealed shut so cold air doesn't escape.

wrong_explanations.A: Magnets don't produce cold — the compressor does.
wrong_explanations.D: Magnets add weight, they don't make things lighter.

subject: Science
question_text: True or False: Rubbing a needle with a magnet in one direction can turn the needle into a magnet.
worked_solution:
Stroking a steel/iron object with a magnet in ONE direction aligns the particles → the object becomes magnetised.
This is how you can make a temporary magnet.
